did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses machine learning algorithms (Support Vector Machine, Random Forest, and Gaussian Naïve Bayes) combined with feature extraction techniques (GLCM, HOG, LBP) to classify skin cancer (Melanoma vs. Nevus) using the ISIC-2019 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the combination of feature extraction methods (GLCM, HOG, LBP) and machine learning algorithms (SVM, Random Forest, Gaussian Naïve Bayes) for skin cancer classification. It details specific research methods, including dataset preprocessing (ISIC-2019), feature extraction techniques, and classification models, and reports new empirical results (e.g., 92% accuracy) rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: COMBINATIONS OF FEATURE EXTRACTIONS AND MACHINE LEARNING ALGORITHMS FOR SKIN CANCER CLASSIFICATION
brief_summary: This study addresses the classification of Melanoma and Nevus skin cancers using the ISIC-2019 dataset, which contained 17,397 images. The researchers combined three feature extraction techniques—Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM), Histogram Oriented Gradients (HOG), and Local Binary Patterns (LBP)—with three machine learning algorithms: Support Vector Machine (SVM), Random Forest (RF), and Gaussian Naïve Bayes (GNB). To handle class imbalance, the Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE) was applied to the dataset. The evaluation results demonstrated that the HOG feature extraction method paired with the SVM algorithm achieved the highest accuracy of 92%. Additionally, combinations of GLCM with HOG, and GLCM with HOG and LBP, when used with the Random Forest algorithm, also achieved an accuracy of 92%. These findings indicate that combining various feature extraction methods with machine learning algorithms can effectively improve skin cancer classification accuracy.

lesion_types_studied.details: Malignant skin cancer class; 12,875 images in the original ISIC-2019 dataset subset used; balanced with Nevus via SMOTE oversampling.
Benign skin lesion class; 4,522 images in the original ISIC-2019 dataset subset used; balanced with Melanoma via SMOTE oversampling.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification between Melanoma and Nevus skin cancer lesions
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly aims to classify skin cancer images into two discrete diagnostic categories: Melanoma (malignant) and Nevus (benign). The study uses machine learning algorithms (SVM, Random Forest, Gaussian Naïve Bayes) to predict these class labels, evaluating performance via accuracy, precision, and F1-score. The target is a discrete label, not a continuous value.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2019
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of skin lesion images derived from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) archive, specifically used for binary classification of Melanoma and Nevus lesions.

use.training: Used for training machine learning models (SVM, Random Forest, Gaussian Naïve Bayes) after preprocessing (inpainting, grayscale conversion, median filtering) and balancing via SMOTE oversampling. The dataset was split into 80% for training.
use.testing: Used for testing model performance. The dataset was split into 20% for testing.
use.other: Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE) was applied to the Nevus class to balance the dataset with the Melanoma class prior to training and testing.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To handle class imbalance in the dataset by increasing the sample size of the minority class (Nevus) to match the majority class (Melanoma).
To divide the dataset into training (80%) and testing (20%) sets for model evaluation.
To evaluate and compare the performance of different combinations of feature extraction methods and machine learning algorithms.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, and F1-score on a train-test split without statistical significance analysis.

ISIC-2019.description: Binary classification of Melanoma (MEL) and Nevus (NV) lesions. Dataset size: 17,397 images (12,875 Melanoma, 4,522 Nevus). Balanced via SMOTE oversampling. Split: 80% training, 20% testing.
ISIC-2019.notes: The highest accuracy of 92% was achieved by HOG with SVM, GLCM+LBP with Random Forest, and GLCM+HOG+LBP with Random Forest. The paper notes that while HOG alone with SVM/RF yielded 92%, combining GLCM with HOG and LBP also matched this performance with Random Forest.

architecture_used.main_model: Traditional Machine Learning Algorithms: Support Vector Machine (SVM), Random Forest (RF), and Gaussian Naïve Bayes (GNB)

HOG.description: Calculates gradient magnitude and orientation for each pixel to describe visual attributes invariant to lighting and background noise.

LBP.description: Assigns binary labels (0 or 1) to pixels based on comparison with neighboring pixels to create texture descriptors.

SVM.description: Creates a hyperplane with maximum margin to separate classes; handles non-linear features.

Random_Forest.description: Ensemble of decision trees trained on random subsets of data and features to reduce overfitting.

Gaussian_Naive_Bayes.description: Probabilistic classifier based on Bayes' theorem assuming normal distribution of attributes.

preprocessing: Median filtering (5x5 mask) for noise removal
training_details.class_imbalance_handling: Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE) applied to Nevus class

preprocessing_techniques: Median filtering (5x5 mask) for noise removal
Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE) for class imbalance handling

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the Nevus (minority) class to balance the dataset with the Melanoma (majority) class prior to training. The method increases the sample size of nevus lesions to a level comparable to that of Melanoma lesions.

conclusions: Combining various feature extraction methods (GLCM, HOG, LBP) with machine learning algorithms (SVM, Random Forest, Gaussian Naïve Bayes) can improve accuracy in skin cancer classification.
The combination of Histogram Oriented Gradients (HOG) feature extraction with the Support Vector Machine (SVM) algorithm achieved the highest accuracy of 92%.
The combination of Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) and Local Binary Patterns (LBP) with the Random Forest algorithm also achieved an accuracy of 92%.
The combination of Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM), Histogram Oriented Gradients (HOG), and Local Binary Patterns (LBP) with the Random Forest algorithm resulted in an accuracy of 92%.
The study contributes to the development of accurate and reliable skin cancer detection, which can aid in early detection and more effective treatment.
The study acknowledges a limitation in that it did not use image segmentation to distinguish skin lesions from other skin lesions, leaving room for future research to achieve better accuracy.
important_remarks: The study lacks image segmentation to distinguish skin lesions from surrounding skin, which is a noted limitation that may affect accuracy and generalizability.

The dataset (ISIC-2019) was artificially balanced using SMOTE oversampling for the minority class (Nevus), which may not reflect real-world clinical prevalence and could lead to overoptimistic performance estimates.
The paper does not report statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) or confidence intervals for the reported accuracy metrics (92%).
The evaluation relies on a single 80/20 train-test split without cross-validation or external validation on independent datasets, limiting the robustness of the findings.
The study is limited to binary classification (Melanoma vs. Nevus) and does not address multi-class skin cancer diagnosis or other lesion types.

Preprocessing steps included inpainting for hair removal and median filtering, but no standardization of image resolution or color space normalization beyond grayscale conversion was explicitly detailed as a rigorous protocol.

The highest accuracy of 92% was achieved by multiple combinations (HOG+SVM, GLCM+LBP+RF, GLCM+HOG+LBP+RF), but the paper does not provide a detailed ablation study or statistical comparison to determine if these differences are significant.
